solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public List<String> topKFrequent(String[] words, int k) {
        Map<String, Integer> freqMap = new HashMap<>();
        for (String word : words) {
            freqMap.put(word, freqMap.getOrDefault(word, 0) + 1);
        }

        PriorityQueue<Map.Entry<String, Integer>> minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
            (a, b) -> a.getValue() == b.getValue() ? b.getKey().compareTo(a.getKey()) : a.getValue() - b.getValue()
        );

        for (Map.Entry<String, Integer> entry : freqMap.entrySet()) {
            minHeap.offer(entry);
            if (minHeap.size() > k) {
                minHeap.poll();
            }
        }

        List<String> result = new ArrayList<>();
        while (!minHeap.isEmpty()) {
            result.add(0, minHeap.poll().getKey());
        }

        return result;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n log(k))
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a min-heap of size k to efficiently track the top k most frequent words. It first counts the frequency of each word using a hash map. Then, it processes each unique word, maintaining a heap that ensures the k most frequent words are retained. Words with the same frequency are ordered lexicographically by using a custom comparator. The result is built by extracting elements from the heap in reverse order. This approach is efficient and meets the follow-up constraints.
